title: Přebuz

infobox.value: ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Frühbuß*) is a town in [Sokolov District](/wiki/Sokolov_District "Sokolov District") in the [Karlovy Vary Region](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary_Region "Karlovy Vary Region") of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). It is the smallest town in the Czech Republic by population, which was 70 inhabitants as of 2025. Before World War II, it was a mining town with 3,000 inhabitants.

["Population of Municipalities – 1 January 2025"](https://csu.gov.cz/produkty/population-of-municipalities-t4l3n8d2iw). [Czech Statistical Office](/wiki/Czech_Statistical_Office "Czech Statistical Office"). 2025-05-16.

**Přebuz** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Frühbuß*) is a town in [Sokolov District](/wiki/Sokolov_District "Sokolov District") in the [Karlovy Vary Region](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary_Region "Karlovy Vary Region") of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). It is the smallest town in the Czech Republic by population, which was 70 inhabitants as of 2025. Before World War II, it was a mining town with 3,000 inhabitants.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=P%C5%99ebuz&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name is probably derived from the Czech name Přebud, meaning "Přebud's (court)". The German name was probably derived from the Czech one.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=P%C5%99ebuz&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Přebuz is located about 21 kilometres (13 mi) north of [Sokolov](/wiki/Sokolov,_Czech_Republic "Sokolov, Czech Republic") and 22 km (14 mi) northwest of [Karlovy Vary](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary "Karlovy Vary"). It lies on the border with Germany, in the western part of the [Ore Mountains](/wiki/Ore_Mountains "Ore Mountains"). The highest point is the mountain Čertova hora at 987 m (3,238 ft) above sea level. The [Rolava](/wiki/Rolava "Rolava") River originates in the municipal territory.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=P%C5%99ebuz&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/P%C5%99ebuz_Kru%C5%A1n%C3%A9_hory.jpg/250px-P%C5%99ebuz_Kru%C5%A1n%C3%A9_hory.jpg)](/wiki/File:P%C5%99ebuz_Kru%C5%A1n%C3%A9_hory.jpg)

View of Přebuz

The foundation of Přebuz is connected with rich finds of [tin ore](/wiki/Cassiterite "Cassiterite"). According to its chronicle from 1836, Přebuz was founded in 1347 by German colonizers. Although this date is unconfirmed, it is considered probable. The first written mention of Přebuz is from 1542. In 1553, Přebuz gained status and rights of a mining town.[[3]](#cite_note-history-3)

Before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the town had about 3,000 inhabitants. From 1938 to 1945, Přebuz was annexed by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and administered as part of the [Reichsgau Sudetenland](/wiki/Reichsgau_Sudetenland "Reichsgau Sudetenland"). After the war, the population has decreased significantly because of [expulsion of Germans](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Germans_from_Czechoslovakia "Expulsion of Germans from Czechoslovakia") and termination of mining.[[3]](#cite_note-history-3)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=P%C5%99ebuz&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Přebuz is by far the least populous municipality with the town status in the Czech Republic.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=P%C5%99ebuz&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Transport")]

There are no railways or major roads passing through the municipal territory.

## Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=P%C5%99ebuz&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/P%C5%99ebuz_kostel_svat%C3%A9ho_Bartolom%C4%9Bje_srpen_2018_%281%29.jpg/250px-P%C5%99ebuz_kostel_svat%C3%A9ho_Bartolom%C4%9Bje_srpen_2018_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:P%C5%99ebuz_kostel_svat%C3%A9ho_Bartolom%C4%9Bje_srpen_2018_(1).jpg)

Church of Saint Bartholomew

The main landmark of Přebuz is the Church of Saint Bartholomew. It is originally a Gothic church from the end of the 14th century, rebuilt in the Baroque style around 1775.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
